action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: stone, bird, river, dog, boat, ship, farmer, spear, tree, road, hunter, hill, lake, king
Adjectives: big, red, tall, blue, old, dark, cold, small
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, hear, feed, carry, see, hold, find, watch
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a stone chase a bird
    six stones chase a bird
    a big stone chase a bird

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the four trees find a tree  =>  kimi guri mo kimiga pe
    the four red ships hold the three small ships  =>  mime pumuga pareru pe keru mo pumuga mumomu pe
    five rivers chase three blue boats  =>  mime gagaga gemuhu peka gi rumiga
    a tree feed the lake  =>  koki pe gafo kimi
    the three ships feed the six roads  =>  fe pafiga pe gafo mime pumuga pe
    five hills hear the three rivers  =>  mime rumiga pe pipi gi kikiga
    the two spears watch the three stones  =>  mime pemoga pe mefe mure horega pe
    two hunters hear four farmers  =>  mo puguriga pipi mure gomega
    four rivers see four old rivers  =>  mo rumiga meru mepu mo rumiga
    the three cold rivers watch the three tall lakes  =>  mime kokiga fegaga pe mefe mime rumiga pahu pe
    two old birds hold five cold stones  =>  gi pemoga pahu keru mure kipokiga meru
    three blue boats carry three red boats  =>  mime gagaga mumomu gugagu mime gagaga gemuhu
    two lakes see the four kings  =>  mo fapuga pe mepu mure kokiga
    three roads chase four big lakes  =>  mo kokiga guhe peka mime pafiga
    the four dark dogs find the six hills  =>  fe kikiga pe guri mo koruga fagoha pe

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a stone chase a bird
2. the four trees find a tree
3. the three ships feed the six roads
4. five rivers chase three blue boats
action.label: response

probes: a stone chase a bird
the four trees find a tree
the three ships feed the six roads
five rivers chase three blue boats

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone chase a bird"  =>  kipoki peka pemo
2. "the four trees find a tree"  =>  kimi guri mo kimiga pe
3. "the three ships feed the six roads"  =>  fe pafiga pe gafo mime pumuga pe
4. "five rivers chase three blue boats"  =>  mime gagaga gemuhu peka gi rumiga
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the first four probes I can infer the language’s core syntax: English SVO becomes <subject> <verb> <object>. The subject is a noun phrase with optional adjectives before the noun, and the object is a noun phrase with optional adjectives before the noun. The language uses a single verb form for the action (e.g., “chase”, “feed”, “see”, “watch”) and marks plurality/count with a number word (two, three, four, five, six) or omits it for singular. Definiteness is marked by the article “the” (definite) or “a” (indefinite singular); indefinite plural nouns omit the article. The language has a fixed word order: article (if present) + number word + adjectives + noun. The informant’s translations show the verb is in the base form (no tense/aspect markers) and the object is a noun phrase with the same article/number/adjective pattern as the subject. I also see that the language uses a single verb for each action (e.g., “chase”, “feed”, “see”, “watch”) and that the verb is in the same form for all of them.

PROBES:
1. the four trees find a tree
2. the three ships feed the six roads
3. five rivers chase three blue boats
4. two hunters hear four farmers

The language uses a single verb form for each action (e.g., “chase”, “feed”, “see”, “watch”) and marks plurality/count with a number word (two, three, four, five, six) or omits it for singular. Definiteness is marked by the article “the” (definite) or “a” (indefinite singular); indefinite plural nouns omit the article. FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the big road hold the five small dogs
2. the three big kings find six red stones
3. the two stones hold five blue hills
4. four small hunters hold a cold farmer
5. two big kings hear the tree
6. two kings find a cold lake
7. five farmers hear a hunter
8. the six farmers watch five old boats
9. four stones carry the three big rivers
10. a red hill carry three spears

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
